464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
## **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member onboarding & first 30 days**: no documented pathway from sign-up through first class, goal-setting, coach assignment, check-in cadence. This is your highest-churn window.
- **Retention mechanics**: no SOP for member check-ins, pause/freeze triggers, win-back outreach, or churn intervention. You're reactive, not proactive.
- **Referral programme**: no structure, incentive rules, tracking, or fulfilment process.
- **Coach quality & consistency**: no audit checklist, class observation protocol, or feedback loop. Coaching variance kills retention.
- **Complaints & incidents**: no escalation path, documentation standard, or resolution timeline. Legal/reputational risk.
- **Supply chain**: "Order Supplies & Sundries" missing — no inventory thresholds, vendor SLAs, or budget guardrails.
- **Member data & GDPR**: no consent capture, retention policy, or data export/deletion workflow. Compliance gap.
- **Finance basics**: no SOP for billing holds, failed payments, refund requests, or pricing changes.
- **Staff scheduling & shift coverage**: no documented protocol for rota planning, absence cover, or handover.

## **Needs updating**

- **Clients (23/33)**: likely have intake & class booking but missing member lifecycle milestones (30-day goals, 90-day outcomes, transformation tracking). No feedback loop into coaching.
- **Coaches (20/24)**: have role basics but no coaching development roadmap (progression, certifications, internal training calendar). No weekly task standardisation.
- **Marketing (5/5)**: may be missing performance tracking SOP, content calendar lock, or campaign post-mortems.
- **General (1/1)**: almost certainly vague. Add org-wide

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO RECOMMENDATIONS

## INCREASE REVENUE

- **Pricing power exists but is untapped.** ARR/M has collapsed from £170 in December to £120 in June — a 29% drop despite member count rising 18% (85→147). You've grown headcount but discounted heavily or added lower-tier memberships. Audit your current pricing tiers and introduce a premium "unlimited + PT" or "peak hours" tier at £25–30/month premium. Target: restore ARR/M to £160+ within two months.

- **June revenue fell 12% month-on-month (£20,199 → £17,711) while churn hit 0% in June but active members rose to 147.** You gained 25 net members but lost £2,487 in revenue — classic sign of acquisition at low price. Shift Q3 focus: retain the 147 through engagement (challenge classes, milestone badges, referral bonuses) rather than chase new low-margin sign-ups. Target: hold churn <2% and grow revenue 8% to £19,100+ by August.

- **Non-peak hours are still soft.** Morning and midweek classes likely have spare capacity in your larger studio. Run a 4-week "Lunchtime Lite" promo (£5 drop-in or included in membership) to fill 9am–12pm slots. Track attendance by class; convert high-attenders to full membership upsells within 3 weeks.

## REDUCE EXPENSES

- **Non-people costs spiked Jan–April (£8,166 → £30,511) and have not fully normalized.** May–June sit at £9,233–£9,390. Audit: utilities/lease/insurance/supplies. The new studio is twice the size — some cost increase is expected — but are you paying double for cleaning, utilities, or software? Identify the £1,500–£2,000 of post-relocation waste and eliminate by end of July. Target: cap non-people costs at £8,500/month.

- **Payroll has crept from £2,514 (Nov–Dec) to £3,686 (June, +46%).** Relocation added 1–2 staff, which is justified for a larger facility. Confirm headcount and utilization: are all instructors fully booked? If not, move one to part-time or gig-based (pay-per-class). Target: hold payroll at £3,300–£3,400 unless revenue crosses £22k/month.

## IMPROVE PROFITABILITY

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **Redistribute Grace's Build: Lower (17 sessions) to JoJo and Eilis.** JoJo already runs Build: Upper heavily; add Lower to create a complete strength programme under one coach's quality control. Eilis can co-lead or shadow to build capability toward Tuesday readiness.

• **Move Grace's Elevate (6 sessions) and Strongevity (3 sessions) to Eilis.** These are lower-intensity classes where Eilis can build confidence and teaching variety without the technical demands of Tuesdays. This brings Eilis's class load up and reduces Grace to zero.

• **Cover Grace's No Sweat Intro (3 sessions) with a rotating coach schedule.** Intros don't require depth; rotate JoJo and Eilis to spread goodwill and free JoJo from single-handedly onboarding new members.

• **JoJo is over-loaded at 133 sessions.** After taking Build: Lower from Grace, assign her Circuits as a fixed weekly slot (currently ad hoc) so members know when to book, and audit her PT waitlist—consider whether all 44 PT slots are essential or if some should move to Eilis.

• **Eilis is under-used at 48 sessions and ready for growth.** Target 70–80 sessions by June by adding: Grace's Elevate and Strongevity, co-leading Build: Lower with JoJo, and a share of PT and Intros. This accelerates her confidence and reduces dependency on JoJo.

• **Strip Body Composition scans from coaching allocation.** They're admin, not coaching. Assign a duty rota (receptionist or any coach with 5 min) and remove from workload counts—frees ~40 'session slots' across the team for real coaching gaps.

c17ea6e513949b5bd341.text:
**For quiet studio time:**

1. **Add a 30-min "Open Gym + Coach" slot on Monday and Thursday evenings** (your quietest days) — members pay €5-8 to access the studio with a coach available for form checks or self-directed work, filling dead time between classes without cannibalizing bookings.

2. **Run a paid 4-week "Engine Fundamentals" workshop on Saturday mornings** (quietest day, lowest attendance class) — rebrand Engine as a beginner-friendly, coach-led intro series rather than a standalone class, targeting newer members who find the full class intimidating.

**For quieter classes themselves:**

3. **Move Barbelle to Wednesday or Friday at 6:45pm** (your peak days/times) and pair it with a one-week "bring a friend free" promotion — it's nearly dead at 34 attendees, so timing + social proof could unlock a hidden audience already in the gym for Build classes.

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

• **Start with your revenue math, not your feelings** — List every service you *currently* deliver (classes, 1-on-1 coaching, nutrition plans, assessments, etc.). Calculate what each generates monthly. Kill or bundle anything generating <£200/month unless it's a lead magnet. This forces ruthless clarity.

• **Build two separate docs with one source of truth** — Create a master spreadsheet (internal) with costs, margins, and decision logic. Export clean versions for coaches (what to sell, how to explain value) and members (benefits-focused, no pricing confusion). One source prevents contradiction.

• **Price by outcome bracket, not time** — Don't say "6 sessions = £X." Say "Build strength foundation: £X/month includes 2 classes + form check-in" and "Transform body: £X/month includes unlimited classes + 1-on-1 + nutrition." Members buy results, not hours. This also stops coaches negotiating.

• **Get coaches to pressure-test it this week** — Show them the draft menu tomorrow. Ask: "What questions will members ask you about this?" and "What's missing?" You'll catch pricing gaps and positioning issues before launch. Their feedback is gold.

• **Publish it everywhere simultaneously** — Website, Instagram stories, printed in studio, email to current members. Announce the menu change as "clarity we're giving you" not "new pricing." Gives members time to adjust and coaches a unified script.

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
**Insights**

1. **You've cracked lead generation but broken conversion.** July's 90 leads at £3/lead is exceptional—a 67% drop in cost-per-lead versus June, and your best volume since April. But the 8% close rate is a crater. April closed at 29%, May at 61%, June at 31%. Something in your funnel—follow-up, lead quality, or sales process—has degraded. You're buying cheap volume that isn't converting.

2. **The math is now working against you.** July's £1,082 profit on £241 spend is solid ROI, but it's misleading: you're only closing 7 from 90 leads. At April's 29% close rate, those 90 leads would've yielded 26 sales and £4,500+ profit. You're leaving £3,400+ on the table because conversion has collapsed, not because of ad spend.

3. **Lead quality has tanked, or your sales follow-up has.** The drop in close rate lines up exactly with the explosion in lead volume. You're either attracting the wrong audience (time-wasters, wrong demographics) or your sales team can't handle the volume and is failing to nurture properly.

**Actions**

1. **Audit your sales process immediately.** Before touching ads, call your sales team: Are they following up within 24 hours? What's the objection pattern? Are July leads less qualified (wrong age, fitness level, location)? Get specifics. The ad isn't broken; the handoff is. If follow-up lag is the issue, throttle ad spend back to 70 leads/month until process is fixed.

2. **Test tighter audience targeting to recover quality.** Your July CPL drop suggests you've either broadened targeting or removed negative filters. Re-segment: test age cohorts (25–45 vs 35–50), exclude past non-converters, use lookalike audiences from April/May converters only, layer in income/education interests. Run three tight segments at £60/week each. Measure close rate, not just CPL.

3. **Run a creative refresh on the winning July ads.** You've found a cheap-lead

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# The Forge — Fractional CFO Action Plan

## REVENUE — BIGGEST ISSUE

• **Revenue/member has collapsed to £111 (June) vs £138 (April) — a 20% drop.** You've added 20 members (122 → 142) but revenue is flat (£16.9k → £15.8k). Your pricing or mix has weakened. **Immediate action:** audit what new members are signing up for — are they on introductory rates, off-peak tiers, or group packages that undercut your standard rate? Lock in a minimum price floor of £130/member and stop discounting to fill seats. Freeze intro offers now.

• **Churn spiked to 12.7% in May and 11.8% in December.** These aren't seasonal blips — they're retention crises. **Action:** run exit surveys on those 18 members who left in May (12.7% of 138). Identify the pain point — class quality, facilities post-move, staff, or price sensitivity. Fix it within 4 weeks or you'll lose another cohort.

• **June net cash is -£2.5k despite £15.8k revenue.** Your cash out (£20.6k) is 131% of revenue. **Check:** are you paying suppliers monthly in arrears? Can you negotiate 45-day terms to flatten the cash cycle? More immediately, what's in that £20.6k? If it's staffing, see expenses below.

---

## EXPENSES — SECOND PRIORITY

• **Cash out has been volatile: £16.5k (Feb) → £39k (Apr, planned relocation) → £20.6k (June).** Excluding April, June is your highest run-rate expense month. **Action:** line-by-line P&L needed urgently — break down payroll, rent (post-move), utilities, supplies, card processing fees. If payroll is >60% of revenue (£9.5k+), you're over-staffed for 142 members; benchmark is 50–55% for a gym your size. Are you running more classes post-relocation than members demand? Cut underattended sessions (track attendance in TeamUp) and move staff to part-time or commission-only for off-peak slots.

• **Card processing fees (Stripe/GoCardless) are baked into that cash-out.** At £15.8k revenue, you're likely paying 2–2.5% (£316–£395/month). **Action:** lock Stripe's volume discount tier now — you should be at 1.8% or lower. That saves
